coredata.dc:title: Rational design of a sandwiched structure Ni(OH)2 nanohybrid sustained by amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots for outstanding capacitance
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Lingxia
Guan, Lingtong
Song, Jianlan
Zheng, Huajun
coredata.dc:description: The rational structure design of nanohybrids plays a vital role in the supercapacitor performance of electrode materials. Herein, a novel sandwiched structure based on Ni(OH)2 and amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs) is prepared via a facile green strategy. The layered Ni(OH)2 nanosheets are spaced by amino groups from af-GQDs to afford a sandwiched configuration with an enlarged pore diameter of 1.8 nm, which leads to the decrease of random aggregation of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets and dramatically facilitates the ion transport, thus contributing to the increased surface area and improved electrochemical capacitance. Besides, the introduction of af-GQDs leads to enhanced electrical conductivity and better cycling stability as well as contributing additional electrochemical double-layer capacitance, resulting in an outstanding specific capacitance of 2653 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. An asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) based on Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs and electrochemical-exfoliated graphene (EG) is assembled, exhibiting a wide potential window range, a high energy density, and an excellent cycling stability with 90.15% capacitance retention. These results definitely demonstrate the great potential of Ni(OH)2-based hybrid materials in the development of high-performance supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ni(OH)2 nanosheets
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Sandwiched structure
Surface area
Nanohybrids
Asymmetric supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of af-GQDs and electrochemical-exfoliated graphene (EG)
Synthesis of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid
Electrochemical characterization in a three-electrode cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of af-GQDs, Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid, and (b) FTIR spectra of af-GQDs and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid.

The characterization results of af-GQDs. (a,b) HRTEM images and (c) the particle size distribution. (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum (red line) and PL spectrum (blue line) under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, (e) PL spectrum at different excitation wavelengths and (f) Raman spectrum. Insets in (d) display the photographs of af-GQDs aqueous solution under light and UV lamp.
TEM images of (a, b) Ni(OH)2 and (c, d) Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid at different magnifications.

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid, inset shows the corresponding pore size distribution; (b) Scheme of the sandwich structure Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid with amino-groups from af-GQDs supported in-between the layered Ni(OH)2 nanosheets.
XPS spectra of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid: (a) survey scan, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) Ni 2p high resolution spectrum.

(a) CV curves of various samples at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1, and (b) GCD curves at different current densities. (c) Comparison of specific capacitance for Ni(OH)2 nanosheets and four different material ratios of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid electrodes at different current densities. (d) GCD curves of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(50) hybrid at different current densities.

The electrochemical properties of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs//EG ASC. (a) CV curves of EG and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs at the scan rate of 20 mV s−1. (b) CV curves at a sweep rate of 50 mV s−1. GCD curves (c) at 5 A g−1 within different potential windows and (d) at different current densities.

(a) Specific capacitance at different current densities, (b) Ragone plots, and (c) cycling performance at 5 A g−1 of the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs//EG ASC. (d) Nyquist plots of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs, EG electrodes and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs//EG ASC. Insets in (c) are the initial and last 10 cycles of GCD curves.
Preparation procedure of af-GQDs, EG paper and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid.
Comparison of the electrochemical performances of the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid in this work and other related electrode materials in literatures.

head.ce:title: Rational design of a sandwiched structure Ni(OH)2 nanohybrid sustained by amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots for outstanding capacitance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry Synthesis Technology, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry Synthesis Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry Synthesis Technology, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry Synthesis Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational structure design of nanohybrids plays a vital role in the supercapacitor performance of electrode materials. Herein, a novel sandwiched structure based on Ni(OH)2 and amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs) is prepared via a facile green strategy. The layered Ni(OH)2 nanosheets are spaced by amino groups from af-GQDs to afford a sandwiched configuration with an enlarged pore diameter of 1.8 nm, which leads to the decrease of random aggregation of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets and dramatically facilitates the ion transport, thus contributing to the increased surface area and improved electrochemical capacitance. Besides, the introduction of af-GQDs leads to enhanced electrical conductivity and better cycling stability as well as contributing additional electrochemical double-layer capacitance, resulting in an outstanding specific capacitance of 2653 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. An asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) based on Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs and electrochemical-exfoliated graphene (EG) is assembled, exhibiting a wide potential window range, a high energy density, and an excellent cycling stability with 90.15% capacitance retention. These results definitely demonstrate the great potential of Ni(OH)2-based hybrid materials in the development of high-performance supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para:
Supercapacitors have gained significant attention as one of the most promising devices for next-generation energy storage due to their fast charge-discharge rate, high power density, long cycling life and environmental benignity [1–3]. According to the charge-storage mechanism, they can generally be categorized into electrical double layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors [4,5]. The representative electrode material for EDLCs is carbon, which exhibits an excellent charge-discharge cycling life in excess of 500,000 cycles [6]. In particular, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been widely explored for energy storage applications owing to their good chemical stability, remarkable electrical conductivity and appealing electrochemical properties [7–11]. GQDs are sp
2 hybridized carbon with various edge functional moieties including hydroxyl, carbonyl, amino on their edges, and the rich structural chemistry provides these GQDs with intrinsic chemical and physical properties, contributing to large electrochemical capacitance [12–18]. On the other hand, the specific capacitance (SC) of some transition metal oxides or hydroxide-based pseudocapacitive materials such as RuO2 [19], MnO2 [20,21], Ni(OH)2 [22,23], CoOOH [24,25] and NiCo layered double hydroxides [26], is 10–100 times higher than that of EDLCs, which significantly enhance the energy density as supercapacitors. Among them, Ni(OH)2 has been recognized as a promising electrode material for pseudocapacitor due to its large theoretical SCs (~3100 F g−1), well-defined electrochemical redox behavior, ready availability and high stability in alkaline electrolyte [27–29]. However, the poor electrical conductivity and relatively low cycle retention of Ni(OH)2 based electrode materials enormously impede their electrochemical activity. Thus, they usually suffer from relatively low power density and poor cycling stability, which significantly restricts their practical application. As a result, the development of carbon and Ni(OH)2 based hybrid electrodes may compromise the paradox of two kinds of supercapacitors and overcome the major challenges posed by the low energy density and poor electrical conductivity.

Another feasible strategy to benefit the electrochemical performance is to increase the specific surface area and offer extra permeable channels for electrolyte ions diffusion by a rational structure design at nanometer-scale. For instance, Xu et al. reported a hierarchical three-dimensional (3D) layered double hydroxide nanosheet array/graphene composite with an interesting sandwich structure, which possessed a large specific surface area of 184.7 m2 g−1 and typical mesoporous features, favorably for achieving high pseudocapacitance performance [30]. Xie et al. synthesized a sandwiched film of β-Ni(OH)2/graphene with a unique layer-by-layer characteristic, which exhibited well-ordered heterolayered stacking by β-Ni(OH)2 singer layers and graphene layers with the spacing of 10.3 Å [31]. The ultrathin nanohybrid with open 2D permeable channels maximized the utilization ratio of the active materials and dramatically decreased the diffusion length of electrolyte. Doong et al. demonstrated the development of N-doped GQDs deposited on Fe3O4-halloysite nanotubes as active anode materials for supercapacitor, where plenty of carboxylic functional groups from N-GQDs quickly reacted to form amide bond, leading to the intimate attachment of N-GQDs onto the surface of Fe3O4-halloysite nanotubes, as well as offering abundant electroactive sites for rapid electrons and electrolyte ions transport [32]. In addition, a hybrid material of sandwiched reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and N, S co-doped CQDs was demonstrated to show robust cyclic stability with high energy and power density as the CQDs existed within interlayer of the rGO largely enhanced the accessibility of the charged ions and thus increased the capacitance of the composite [17].

Herein, we present a simple and green approach in designing an interesting sandwich-structured hybrid material of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets/amino-functionalized GQDs (Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs), and the preparation process is illustrated in Scheme 1 (Experimental Section). The layered Ni(OH)2 nanosheets are spaced by -NH2 groups from af-GQDs in a self-assembled manner and the formation mechanism is well elaborated. As a novel electrode material for supercapacitors, the optimal Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid electrode exhibits an outstanding SC, three times as much as that of pure Ni(OH)2 electrode. Meanwhile, the asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) based on Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid and electrochemical-exfoliated graphene (EG), i.e., Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs//EG, exhibits a wide operating voltage window, a high energy density and an excellent cycling stability, indicating the promising application in high performance energy storage devices.

All solvents and reagents were purchased from Aladdin and used without further purification. The ultrapure water of 18.2 MΩ by a Hitech-Sciencetool (Master-Q) was utilized to prepare all the solutions. All the experiments were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of af-GQDs and electrochemical-exfoliated graphene (EG)
ce:section.ce:para: The af-GQDs and EG were achieved via an electrochemical approach in an aqueous media (Scheme 1). Typically, the electrolysis of the graphite rods was performed on a CHI760D electrochemical work station (CH Instruments, Chenhua Shanghai, China) in 50 mL of 0.1 M (NH4)2SO4 aqueous solution (pH = 5.5), and two high purity graphite rods (99.999%) were used as anode and cathode with a distance of 2 cm. New graphite rods were under pre-treatment by applying a bias of 10 V for 30 min and the surface of graphite rods undergone erosion. Then, 2 mL of ammonia (25–28%) were added into the electrolyte at 10 V for 60 min to obtain af-GQDs solution and the color of the electrolyte turned to dark-yellow gradually. Eventually, the mixed solution was collected and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to remove the insoluble particles. In order to get a pure GQD solution, the obtained solution was dialyzed over deionized water in a dialysis bag (retain molecular weight 3500 Da) for 48 h. Meanwhile, the bottom graphite mixture particles were selected from the precipitation and subsequently re-dispersed in DMF for a week. The top suspension was selected and filtered through a 0.2 μm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane by vacuum filtration and washed repeatedly with deionized water to remove the residual DMF. The obtained graphene paper was dried in an electric oven under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: Ni(OH)2 nanosheets were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Typically, 20 mL of DMF with 2 mL of 0.2 M Ni(OAc)2 were heated at 80 °C under stirring for 1 h. Then, 20 mL of deionized water was added and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, followed by heating in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After reaction, Ni(OH)2 were obtained by centrifugation, and the green powders were washed by deionized water and dried in an oven under vacuum at 60 °C. Finally, 30–60 mg of Ni(OH)2 powders were added into 20 mL of af-GQDs solution (1 mg mL−1), and the mixed solution was kept stirring and ultrasonicated for 60 min to produce uniform mixture at room temperature, then it was freeze-dried to yield the gray-green powders Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid (Scheme 1).

The morphology and microstructure of these composites were characterized by using a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL Hitachi S-4700, Japan) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM200CX, JEOL). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation) experiments were performed to study the crystallographic information of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was performed using an ESCALab MKII spectrometer with Al Kα (1.4866 keV) as the X-ray source. The Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra and the fluorescence spectra were obtained on a UV-2700 spectrometer and a RF-6000 spectrometer, respectively. The components of materials were measured by a Nicolet IS10 spectrometer FT-IR instrument and a Horiba Jobin Yvon Lab RAM Raman instrument. The specific surface area and pore size distribution were investigated by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (Quantachrome NOVA 1000e instruments).

Electrochemical properties were characterized by a CHI760D electrochemical workstation in 1 M KOH solution as the electrolyte with a three-electrode system where the Pt and Hg/HgO were used as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. The Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid as the working electrode was prepared by mixing 80 wt% the as-prepared sample with 10 wt% acetylene black and 10 wt% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder together. Then drops of N-Methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) were used to form slurries thoroughly. After drying in an oven under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h, the mixture was pressed onto nickel foam current collectors (1 × 1 cm2). Similarly, EG paper electrodes were prepared by pressing onto the nickel foam current collectors. CV, EIS, and GCD were carried out to examine the electrochemical properties. The EIS measurements were preformed over a frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz at an amplitude of 5 mV. The capacitive performance of the electrodes in a two-electrode configuration was also discussed by CV and GCD, and a LAND battery system was used to investigate the cycling stability.

The specific capacitance of different electrodes can be calculated according to the following Eq. (1):
(1)
C =
2
i m
∫ Vdt
V 2
Vi Vf
where C is the specific capacitance, i
m is the discharge current density, V
f is the maximum potential (0.55 V), V
i is minimum potential (0.0 V), and ∫Vdt is the integral area of the discharge curves.

To balance the charge storage between positive electrode and negative electrode in the asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) assembled by using the optimal Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid as the positive electrode and EG paper as the negative electrode [33,34], the mass of the positive and negative electrodes was calculated according to Eq. (2):
(2)
q +
=
q −
,
m +
m −
=
Cs −
∆
V −
Cs +
∆
V +
where q and m are the charge and mass of the electrode, respectively. C
s and △V are the specific capacitance and potential window, respectively, for the positive and negative electrodes. The energy and power densities of the ASC were calculated according to Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively:
(3)
E =
C ∆
V 2
2 × 3.6
(4)
P = E ×
3600
∆ t
where △t is the discharge time of the ASC.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns reveal the structure information of as-prepared af-GQDs, Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid and the results are displayed in Fig. 1a. Only a broad peak located at 23.5°corresponding to (002) plane of the graphene-sheet stacking peak is observed for af-GQDs, suggesting the presence of the reduced graphene oxide [35]. However, a specific and sharp diffraction peak assigned to the typical 0.334 nm interlayer spacing of original graphite (2θ around 26°) [30,36] is missing, possibly due to the -NH2 groups modified on the surface of GQDs. Five characteristic peaks at 12.1, 24.6, 33.3, 36.4 and 59.4° corresponding to (003), (006), (101), (015) and (110) diffraction planes of hexagonal α-Ni(OH)2 (JCPDS 38–715), respectively, are clearly observed for Ni(OH)2 nanosheets sample, which is in good agreement with previous literature [37]. For Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid, the high crystalline nature of α-Ni(OH)2 does not change but without the feature for af-GQDs, which might be because of the small diameter of af-GQDs (which will be verified by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images in Fig. 2a–c) and the low loading amount that are under the detection limit of the equipment (content <1%, size below 5 nm). To further verify the complexation effect between Ni+ and amino-groups from af-GQDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed and the result is shown in Fig. 1b. The broad bands at 3300 and 1610 cm−1 correspond to the NH in-plane stretching vibration and out-of-plane stretching of the amine groups, respectively, for sample af-GQDs. The sharp bands at 1100 and 648 cm−1 are assigned to the CN and -NH2 groups, respectively. These typical features suggest the functionalization of amino-groups in af-GQDs. While for the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid, almost all of the NH and -NH2 stretching vibration peaks disappear, indicating that most of amino-groups are fixed to complex with Ni+ cation. In addition, there is an obvious sharp peak at 3644 cm−1, which is attributed to –OH group in the brucite-like structure [38,39], and the a broad band around 3507 cm−1 is related to the hydroxide vibration of a hydrogen-bonded water molecule which occurs in the interlamellar space of α-Ni(OH)2 [40]. Besides, two bands around 684 and 497 cm−1 are ascribed to the δ Ni-O-H and ν NiO vibrations, respectively [38,41], indicating the successful hybridization of af-GQDs and Ni(OH)2.

The structure morphology and optical properties of af-GQDs are displayed in Fig. 2. As shown in Fig. 2a–c, the af-GQDs are well monodispersed in the aqueous media with a uniform spherical morphology. The in-plane lattice-spacing of 0.33 nm is observed corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of the graphite carbon [42] and the average diameter is c.a. 3.5 nm. A clear peak at 230 nm can be observed in the UV–visible spectrum of af-GQDs sample, as shown in the spectrum I (red line) in Fig. 2d, and the maximal absorbance can be ascribed to π-π* transition of CC domain in the graphene structure [43]. The maximum emission peak of the as-prepared GQDs is located at 455 nm in the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum (blue line, Fig. 2d), and the aqueous suspension of af-GQDs exhibits blue luminescence (insets in Fig. 2d) under irradiation from a UV lamp (365 nm). Interestingly, the PL emission peaks are red-shifted with the increasing excitation wavelengths from 340 to 400 nm (Fig. 2e), indicating that the af-GQDs possess the excitation-wavelength-dependence feature, which is in accordance with the previous reports [8,44,45]. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 2f demonstrates the characteristic peaks of af-GQDs where the D band and G band are centered at around 1340 and 1600 cm−1, respectively. The D-band is assigned to disordered sp
3 carbon while G-band is attributed to the ordered sp
2 carbon [46]. The small 2D-band suggests the existence of few-layers graphene [47]. Additionally, the intensity ratio of D-band and G-band (ID/IG) is lower than 1, indicating that the functionalization and defects are existed at graphene edges [48].

The morphology of pure Ni(OH)2 sample is revealed in Fig. 3a and b, which shows a sheet-like structure and these nanosheets tend to stack with each other. While for the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid (Fig. 3c and d), some af-GQDs are tiled on the surface of the Ni(OH)2 nanosheets, and others are incorporated into the nanosheets, which indicates an effective prevention of the Ni(OH)2 nanosheets from aggregation by the integration of af-GQDs and Ni(OH)2. More direct evidences about the layered Ni(OH)2 nanosheets sustained by af-GQDs can be derived from the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) results (Fig. 4a). The isotherm for Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid can be ascribed to type-IV with H3 hysteresis loop in the IUPAC classification, suggesting the existence of mesopores and flat-slit-like pores. Interestingly, the BET specific surface area for Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid is tested to be 84.6 m2 g−1, which is close to twice the value of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets sample (49.8 m2 g−1). The pore size distribution was calculated from the desorption curves by the Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, which is mainly between 1.0 and 4.0 nm, and centered at about 2.4 nm for Ni(OH)2 nanosheet sample. While the pore sizes for Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid are obviously enlarged, distributing between 3.8 and 6.2 nm, and the maximum pore size distributes at 4.0 nm. Compared with Ni(OH)2 nanosheet sample, the enlarged pore diameter d of 1.8 nm is from the space for -NH2 functionalized GQDs to connect with layered Ni(OH)2 nanosheets in a self-assembled manner, leading to the formation of sandwiched structured Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid (Fig. 4b). The increased specific surface area and the mesopores definitely provide better penetration of the electrolyte and more electroactive sites for redox reactions.

Fig. 5 displays the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results of the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid. The survey spectrum (Fig. 5a) mainly shows C, N, O and Nil species in the hybrid. The peak located at 284.4 eV in the C 1s high resolution XPS spectrum is ascribed to the characteristic peak of C 1s, which can be divided into three peaks corresponding to sp
2 carbons (C=C/C-C, 284.6 eV), sp
3 carbons (CN, 285.4 eV) and carbonyl carbons (CO, 288.4 eV) (Fig. 5b). The nearly disappeared epoxide groups at 286.4 eV possibly suggests the presence of nucleophilic reaction with ammonia [49] during the electrochemical exfoliation process, thus transforming those above groups into amino groups (-NH2), which is in accordance with the results from N 1s high resolution spectrum in Fig. 5c. The N 1s peak can be divided into two peaks at 399.7 and 401.2 eV representing amino N and amide N, respectively [50,51]. In addition, the Ni 2p XPS spectrum shows two major peaks centered around 874.1 and 856.4 eV with a spin-energy separation of 17.7 eV (Fig. 5d), corresponding to Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2, respectively, accompanied by extra two sets of peaks located at higher binding-energy sides being related with their satellite peaks, which is the features for Ni(OH)2 and in accordance with previous report [52]. The results are also in good agreement with the XRD and FT-IR results in Fig. 1.

Based on the above results, a possible formation mechanism of the af-GQDs during the electrochemical exfoliation process is proposed (Scheme S1). The electrochemical exfoliation graphite is mainly based on the anodic oxidation of water and the intercalation of electrolyte anions between the graphite sheets, and it consists of four steps: (i) Electrochemical oxidative cleavage of the graphite anode, where the O and OH radicals produced by anodic oxidation of water can serve as electrochemical “scissors” [53–55] to cut carbon nanocrystals and form oxygenated groups. (ii) Oxidation process to open the boundary layer at the edge of the area, which is advantageous to the intercalation of OH− and SO4 2− anions [48], making the graphite anode depolarization and expansion, forming the graphene oxidized sheets (GOSs). (iii) The formation of the af-GQDs can occur through a cutting route along the edge of the sp
2 cluster structure in the GOSs. A linear arrangement of epoxy bridges causes stronger strain in the C-O-C tricyclic-ring which can result in the break of the CC bonds. (iv) The nucleophilic substitution reaction with ammonia [49] to generate af-GQDs fragments, meanwhile the presence of NH4 + would also interact with the defect sites in graphene sheets and amino groups tend to terminate the dangling bonds on the edge sites of graphene sheets [50], thus leading to the formation of af-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization in a three-electrode cell
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical properties of as-prepared Ni(OH)2 nanosheets sample and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid were evaluated in a three-electrode cell, and the results are shown in Figs. 6 and S1. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were carried out at a sweep rate of 10 mV s−1 within the potential window from 0 to 0.6 V in 1 M KOH solution to confirm the electrochemical behavior type of electrode materials. A pair of symmetric redox peaks can be clearly observed (Fig. 6a) for both Ni(OH)2 nanosheets electrode and Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid, which implies that the capacitance characteristics are mainly dominated by Faradaic redox mechanism [56]. The conversion reaction in the electrolyte between different nickel oxidation states can be described as the following equation:
α − Ni
OH
2
+
OH −
↔ γ − NiOOH +
H 2
O +
e −

The area of the CV curves for sample prepared using 50 mg Ni(OH)2 nanosheets, denoted as sample Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(50), is larger than that of the other three hybrid samples, showing the largest SC, i.e., they follow the trend: Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(50) > Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(40) > Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(30) > Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(60) > Ni(OH)2. Similarly, the CV curves measured at scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s−1 (Fig. S1) exhibit a pair of symmetric redox peaks on each curve and the shapes change little. With increasing scan rates, the current density increases and the oxidation peaks shift to a more positive position while the reduction peaks shift to a more negative one. Thus, it contributes to an increase of the internal diffusion resistance within the pseudoactive material with increasing scan rates [22,57]. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements at 1 A g−1 within a potential window of 0 to 0.55 V (vs. Hg/HgO) were conducted to further estimate the best ratio of Ni(OH)2 powders to af-GQDs solution (Fig. 6b). The existence of a distinct potential plateau region for all samples are observed, suggesting the Faradic reaction occurring during the charge-discharge process [58,59]. Remarkably, the rate capability of the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(50) electrode is excellent, and the specific capacity retention is 74% when the current density increases from 1 to 10 A g−1. The specific capacitance values of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(30), Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(40), Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(50), Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(60) and Ni(OH)2 are calculated to be 2042, 2352, 2653, 1510 and 856 F g−1, respectively (Fig. 6c). Note that the hybrid sample Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(50) delivers the largest SC, in consistent with the CV results in Fig. 6a. To further explore the electrochemical performance of the hybrid, the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(50) sample was examined by GCD at different current densities as shown in Fig. 6d. The SC values are calculated to be 2653, 2426, 2150, 1948 and 1658 F g−1 at 1, 2, 5, 10 and 20 A g−1, respectively. It is noteworthy that even at 20 A g−1, the SC can be kept as high as 1658 F g−1, much larger than that of pure Ni(OH)2 nanosheets electrode (261 F g−1 at 20 A g−1), indicating that the hybrid has a good rate capability which is quite crucial for the electrode materials in supercapacitors to achieve both high power and energy density. The enhancement is owing to the introduction of af-GQDs to provide ion-transport path and improve the contact surface area between the OH− ions and active species [30]. Moreover, with the increasing current density, all the SC decreases gradually, which may be caused by an increase of the potential drop due to the electrode resistance and relatively insufficient Faradic redox reaction of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs at higher current densities [60]. Table 1 lists the main electrochemical parameters of the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid electrode compared with those of the related electrode materials in literatures. It is obvious that the SC of the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid electrode is comparable or even superior to the best values of the related Ni(OH)2-based counterparts, indicating their potential application in energy-storage systems.

In order to further investigate the properties of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs electrodes in a full-cell configuration, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device was assembled by using the optimal Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid (sample Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs(50)) as the positive electrode and the EG paper as the negative electrode. According to the GCD curve, the SC of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid is 2653 F g−1 while that of EG is 259.6 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 (Fig. S2). The potential windows of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs and EG electrodes are 0 to 0 .6V and −1.0 to 0 V (Fig. 7a), respectively, indicating that the minimum stable potential window for the ASC device is 1.6 V. Fig. 7b displays the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs//EG ASC with different potential windows varying from 0–1.6 V to 0–2.0 V at 50 mV s−1. When the cell voltages reach the value of 2.0 V, there is remarkable change of anodic current [23], indicating that the cell electrolyte is unstable at 2.0 V. Furthermore, the CV curves show two relatively weak redox peaks, revealing synergy contributions from both EDLC from af-GQDs and pseudocapacitance from Ni(OH)2. The GCD curves of the ASC device under different voltage ranges at 5 A g−1 are presented in Fig. 7c. Each GCD curve exhibits a good symmetry, and when the voltage reaches to 1.9 V, there is an obvious plateau compared to others, suggesting that the stable voltage is 1.8 V. The GCD curves for the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs//EG ASC are shown in Fig. 7d. The charging and discharging cycles at various current densities are rather symmetric, demonstrating the excellent capacitive characteristic and high coulombic efficiency of the ideal capacitor.

The SC values are calculated to be 103.3, 83.7, 62.1, 48.5 and 37.5 F g−1 at 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5 and 10 A g−1, respectively, depicting the good rate capability (Fig. 8a). The ASC shows a maximum energy density of 46.5 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1 kW kg−1 and a maximum power density of 9 kW kg−1 at an energy density of 16.8 Wh kg−1 (calculated according to Eqs. (3) and (4), Fig. 8b). The cycling performance of the ASC was also tested by GCD curves at 5 A g−1, and the SC is still stable with a 90.15% retention of the initial value after 3000 cycles (Fig. 8c). The insets display the charge-discharge processes during the initial 10 and last 10 cycles, and the shape changes very little, indicating good stability of the hybrid material. The good electrochemical reversibility and high rate capability can be further explained by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 8d), which shows that all the Nyquist plots present similar shapes with a depressed semicircle at high frequency region and a spike at lower frequency region, revealing the electrodes of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs and EG endow a better electric conductivity. The straight line at lower frequency region corresponds to the diffusive resistance of the electrolyte ion (Warburg resistance). The vertical straight line in low frequency region indicates small diffusive resistance in these electrochemical capacitor, which could be ascribed to the novel sandwiched nanohybrids.

Specifically, such remarkable electrochemical performance can be ascribed to the synergistic effect after integration of af-GQDs and Ni(OH)2 nanosheets, as the existence of af-GQDs increases the electronic conductivity and the employment of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets provides large capacitance. The uniform dispersion of af-GQDs and in situ amino-functionalization at edge sites of graphene sheets are also advantageous to largely employ the capacitance of Ni(OH)2. Moreover, this unique sandwiched structure of Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs can not only reduce the aggregation of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets, but also significantly facilitate the fast charge transport and ion diffusion during the process of charge-discharge. Moreover, the af-GQDs as carbon material can also contribute to the intrinsic capacitance (EDLC) to the whole specific capacitance. Further improvement in electrochemical performance could be achieved by changing the concentration of af-GQDs solution to optimize the hybrid.

In summary, we utilized a facile green method to synthesize a novel sandwiched nanohybrid consisting of layered Ni(OH)2 nanosheets sustained by amino-groups as the linkage from af-GQDs. The formation mechanism is well elucidated. As an electrode material for supercapacitor, the Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs hybrid demonstrates excellent electrochemical performance. The SC is found to be as high as 2653 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in 1 M KOH solution with good rate capability. In addition, an ASC device (Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs//EG) is constructed, achieving a wide operating voltage range (~1.8 V), high energy and power density, as well as excellent cycling stability with a retention of 90.15% after 3000 cycles. These encouraging results provide new insight into rational structure design and fabrication of Ni(OH)2-based hybrid electrode materials for high-performance energy-storage systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51702287), Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY15B030007), Research Projects of Education Department of Zhejiang Province (Y201738057) and Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (2017A610064) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.02.243.
